coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of silver/carbon dot nanoparticles from Malva Sylvestris for fluorescence determination of tetracycline
coredata.dc:creator: Moaddab, Akram
Ghasemi, Shahram
coredata.dc:description: In the present research, silver/carbon dot nanoparticles (Ag/CDs) were used for fluorescence determination of tetracycline (TC). For this purpose, extract of Malva with the scientific name of Malva Sylvestris from Malvaceae family with different medicinal properties and high nutritional value was used as the precursor for CDs synthesis and the reducing agent of silver ions through the hydrothermal method. The effect of synthesis parameters including AgNO3 and β-cyclodextrin concentrations, amount of extract, temperature, pH, and time were investigated on the preparation of Ag/CDs. The X-ray diffraction and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) confirmed the synthesis of Ag/CDs, and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) showed the construction of nanoparticles with an average size of 25 nm. The intensity of localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) absorption peak of silver nanoparticles was increased with addition of TC. In addition, with increasing the concentration of analyte, the fluorescence intensity of the Ag/CDs was enhanced due to metal enhanced fluorescence (MEF). By plotting the calibration curve using fluorescence data, the linear range of 0.025–3 ppm and the limit of detection of 0.6 μg L−1 (1.2 nM) were obtained for the method. To evaluate the practical performance of the sensor, the TC determination was carried out in human blood plasma and satisfactory results were obtained.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Malva
β-Cyclodextrin
Metal enhanced fluorescence
Silver nanoparticles
Tetracycline

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of TC by Ag/CDs in aqueous solution
Characterization of Ag/CDs in aqueous solutions
Optimization of conditions for Ag/CDs synthesis
Stability of the Ag/CDs in aqueous solution
The effect of Ag/CD volume ratio
Optimization of analytical conditions for the determination of TC
Possible mechanism for fluorescence enhancement process
Ag/CDs as a sensor for determination of TC
Determination of TC in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for the green synthesis for Ag/CDs from Malva flowers.

(A) UV absorption and emission spectra of CD and Ag/CDs, (B) Fluorescence spectra of Ag/CDs at different excitation wavelengths, (C) XRD pattern for the Ag/CD and (D) FT-IR spectra of different materials.
(A) FESEM images of Ag/CDs at two different magnifications and, (B) TEM image and (C) EDS spectrum.
(A) Adsorption and (C) fluorescent spectra of solution conataining Ag/CDs in the presence of different TC concentrations. (B) and (D) calibration curves.
Performance data on fluorometric methods for TC determination.
Determination of TC in human blood plasma samples on Ag/CDs.

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of silver/carbon dot nanoparticles from Malva Sylvestris for fluorescence determination of tetracycline

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Chemistry, University of Mazandaran, 47416-95447, Babolsar, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Chemistry, University of Mazandaran, Babolsar 47416-95447, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present research, silver/carbon dot nanoparticles (Ag/CDs) were used for fluorescence determination of tetracycline (TC). For this purpose, extract of Malva with the scientific name of Malva Sylvestris from Malvaceae family with different medicinal properties and high nutritional value was used as the precursor for CDs synthesis and the reducing agent of silver ions through the hydrothermal method. The effect of synthesis parameters including AgNO3 and β-cyclodextrin concentrations, amount of extract, temperature, pH, and time were investigated on the preparation of Ag/CDs. The X-ray diffraction and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) confirmed the synthesis of Ag/CDs, and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) showed the construction of nanoparticles with an average size of 25 nm. The intensity of localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) absorption peak of silver nanoparticles was increased with addition of TC. In addition, with increasing the concentration of analyte, the fluorescence intensity of the Ag/CDs was enhanced due to metal enhanced fluorescence (MEF). By plotting the calibration curve using fluorescence data, the linear range of 0.025–3 ppm and the limit of detection of 0.6 μg L−1 (1.2 nM) were obtained for the method. To evaluate the practical performance of the sensor, the TC determination was carried out in human blood plasma and satisfactory results were obtained.

ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotic is a substance produced by a microbe, kill bacteria and, inhibits pathogenic microorganisms [1]. Antibiotics are mostly used to treat infectious diseases caused by non-viral microbial agents and pathogenic bacteria [2]. TC is an important member of widely used antibiotics which is produced through the Streptomyces action [3]. It is active against gram-negative and gram-positive bacteria, as well as microorganisms such as mycoplasmas, chlamydia, and rickettsia. TC molecule can bind to the microbial ribosome to prevent protein synthesis [4,5], and therefore is widely used in additives and drugs [6]. On the other hand, excessive consumption of it may cause bacterial resistance and possible side effects in the human body so there is an urgent need for sensitive and selective optical probes to determine its low concentrations in aqueous solutions [7].

So far, there are various analytical and conventional techniques such as capillary electrophoresis (CE), spectrophotometry, high-efficiency liquid chromatography (HPLC), chemiluminescence, and mass spectrometry (MS) for TC determination. Notably, these techniques have certain disadvantages such as the use of expensive and sophisticated equipment. In addition, the sampling methods are time-consuming and tedious. Recently, fluorescence has gained great attention among the other analytical techniques owing to its relatively fast, sensitive, and low-cost features for measuring and detecting low-concentration of analytes, especially in the presence of other samples. TC molecules produce fluorescent response due to their conjugated structure which makes possible their determination in real samples. However, due to low quantum performance, it is necessary to enhance the intensity of fluorescence and sensitivity for further studies in aqueous solutions [8–10].

Carbon dots (CDs) are a new member of carbon-based nanomaterials [11]. They have received more attention due to their special advantages such as low toxicity, easy preparation and low production cost, water-solubility, chemical durability, luminosity and, high quantum performance [12,13]. As an electron receiver or donor, CDs are used in photoelectric sensors, catalysis, and analytical sensors due to their electrochemiluminescence and chemical fluorescence [14,15].

The interactions between some noble metal nanoparticles such as silver nanoparticles and fluorophores, which have the ability to increase the intensity of luminescence, are named metal-enhanced fluorescence (MEF) [16]. It is a well-known method that has attracted a lot of attention due to its widespread use in molecular fluorescence assessment, medical diagnosis, biosensors, cell imaging, and measurement. The use of metal surfaces in the colloidal aqueous solution can alter the spectral characteristics of fluorophores [17–20]. MEF is based on the fact that metal nanoparticles can emit electromagnetic radiation at appropriate wavelengths from conduction band electrons. The coordinated and cumulative oscillations of metal electrons stimulated by radiant rays are known as localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) which excites the flexible electromagnetic field around the surface of the nanoparticles [21]. The electric field generated by the metal can interact with the excited fluorophore and changes its emission. These interactions are essentially two-way.

The size, shape, type, and composition of metal nanoparticles, as well as the specific spacing between them and fluorophores, are very effective in increasing their emission intensity [22–24]. To date, several metals have been used for MEF applications including silver, copper, gold, and aluminum. The transfer of non-radiant energy between them depends not only on the strength of the electric field but also on the extent of the spectral overlap between the metal surface and the fluorophore. When the adsorption spectra of nanoparticles overlap with fluorophore, the rate of excitation and the rate of fluorophore emission increase. Therefore, a possible explanation for the increase in fluorescence may be through fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism [25–29].

In this work, Ag nanoparticles decorated on CDs were prepared to investigate the effect of MEF in improving TC determination. The hydrothermal synthesis authorized us to generate different kinds of colloidal metallic nanoparticles with size manageable nanoparticles [30,31]. In this regard using “green chemistry” for the first time, a one-step hydrothermal synthesis was designed to fabricate CDs with suitable fluorescent properties from extract of Malva plant with scientific name of Malva Sylvestris belongs to Malvaceae family. During the hydrothermal preparation of Ag/CDs, extract was used as both reducing agent of silver ions and the precursor for preparation of CDs.

It was previously reported that the presence of hydroxyl groups in polyvinylpyrrolidone can reduce Ag+, Pd4+, and Au3+ ions to their respective metals [32]. Likewise, the hydroxyl groups present in polysaccharides and anthocyanin were also be used for the reduction of Au3+ to Au0. In this case, Malva extract, which is rich in polysaccharides and anthocyanins, was used in the presence of β-cyclodextrin stabilizer to reduce Ag+ to Ag0. Then the ability of Ag/CDs to determine TC in aqueous media was investigated by the fluorescence method.

Malva plant was gathered from Kashmar areas (Razavi Khorasan province, Iran). All chemicals used in this work including silver nitrate (AgNO3, purity 99.85%), β-cyclodextrin (β-CD) ((C6H10O5)7, 98%), hydrochloric acid (37%), sodium hydroxide (99%), Tris buffer (Tris(hydroxymethyl) aminomethane and C4H11NO3, 99%) were of analytical grade and purchased from Merck. Tetracycline (TC, C22H24N2O8.HCl) was purchased from Iran Drug Corporation and used without further purgation. The samples of human blood plasma were obtained from medical laboratories at a local hospital. The TC solutions were prepared through diluting the stock solution (1000 ppm) in distilled water at the beginning of each experiment. In addition AgNO3 (5 mM) solution was prepared freshly. The prepared solutions were stored in darkish volumetric flasks at 5 °C and twisted by aluminum foil to prevent their possible damage from exposure to light.

UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded in the range of 200–800 nm with double-beam spectroscopy (Jasco V-750, Japan). Fluorescence spectrum and emission intensity were evaluated using a spectrofluorometer (Jasco FP-8300, Japan) with a xenon lamp as the excitation source and 10 nm slit width. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) measurements were taken using Bruker spectrophotometer in the range of 400–4000 cm−1. The size and morphology of the sample was investigated using transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Philips EM 208S) at a voltage of 100 kV. The morphology and composition of the sample were studied using a field emission scanning electron microscopy–energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (FESEM–EDS, TESCAN MIRA III). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was also recorded using a diffractometer (Philips PW1730) with a Cu Kα exciting source (λ = 1.54056 Å).

The extraction method was performed according to the previously reported procedure [33]. At first, petals were rinsed thoroughly with distilled water and then dried at room temperature. 0.09 g of dried petals was dispersed in 30 mL distilled water (0.003 g mL−1) in a beaker. Later, the solution was stead in the microwave oven at the power of 360 W for 30 s to perform the extraction. Finally, the mixture was filtered with Whatman paper (grade 42) for the removal of coarse particles from the liquid phase. Then, extract was stored at 5 °C for further analysis.

One-step hydrothermal method was used for the green synthesis of the colloidal Ag/CDs using Malva Sylvestris extract. Briefly, 1 mL of the extracted solution (0.003 g mL−1, 25 mL) was diluted with distilled water and transferred to a 50 mL beaker, and then β-CD (0.5 g mL−1) was added as the protecting agent to stabilize Ag/CDs. The mixture was slowly stirred for 5 min using a magnetic stirrer, and then 1 mL of AgNO3 (5 mM) was added to the aqueous solution. After stirring for 20 min, the obtained mixture was transferred to a 25 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and was heated at 100 °C for 2.5 h. Then, the mixture was cooled to ambient temperature and centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 15 min to remove the coarse particles. The colloidal solution was then transferred to a dark container and stored at 5 °C for further characterization. The illustration for the green preparation of Ag/CDs is shown in Fig. 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of TC by Ag/CDs in aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the effect of Ag/CDs on flouresence intensity of TC, different volume ratios (0, 5 10, 25, 40, 70, and 100%) were prepared from the stock solution. Then, 1 mL of TC solution (1000 ppm, 10 mL) was added to each of them and diluted with distilled water to 10 mL. Each mixture was equilibrated for 15 min at ambient temperature. Then, each of them was transferred to the quartz cell and the emission spectra were recorded using spectrofluorometer. Furthermore, the changes in blue emission of the solutions under UV light illumination were examined at 365 nm [34].

In the next step, the Ag/CDs concenteration was fixed at volume ratio of 5 %. Various concentrations of TC were prepared in the range of 2 – 400 ppm. Then, 100 μL of each solution was added into the Ag/CDs solutions. The final mixture was diluted with 10 mL of Tris buffer (0.01 M, pH = 9.0) and mixed at room temperature for 15 min. Then, absorption spectra were recorded with UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Jasco V730, Japan). Subsequently, the fluorescence intensity of the solutions was measured in the range of 390–650 nm with an excitation wavelength of 370 nm.

The fluorescence intensity of Ag/CDs solution was measured in the absence (F0) and presence of different concentrations of TC (F). The maximum fluorescence intensity of solution was measured at 540 nm and F/F0 was calculated to evaluate the performance of Ag/CDs in the determination of TC concentration.

The fluorescence quantum yield of Ag/CD was determined by comparing the fluorescence intensity and the adsorption values against the reference quinine sulfate using the previously reported method [35] according to the following equation:
Q = QStd .F/FStd .A/AStd.ɳ2 /ɳ2 Std(1)
where A and AStd are the absorbance of sample and standard at the excitation wavelength, respectively. F and FStd are the fluorescence intensity values of Ag/CDs and standard, respectively. ɳ is the solvent refractive index in which quinine sulfate is dissolved as a reference (QStd = 0.54) in 0.1 M H2SO4 (ɳ = 1.33), and the Ag/CDs were dissolved in deionized water (ɳ = 1.33). From the equation, a quantum yield of 9.92% was obtained for Ag/CDs.

Firstly, 10 mL of blood plasma sample was added to 10 mL of 8% (w/v) trichloroacetic acid to remove proteins and lipids from the plasma samples. The mixture was centrifuged for 15 min at 5000 rpm to separate the coarse particles from the solution. The supernatant phase was filtered by a 0.45 μm membrane. Prior to analysis, the prepared plasma was diluted by adding Tris buffer (pH = 9.0, 0.01 M). Then, 4 mL of Ag/CDs solutions with a volume ratio of 5% were poured into three flasks and reached to a volume of 10 mL using diluted plasma. 100 μL of TC (100 ppm) was added and the solutions were mixed well. Then, the fluorescence intensity of each solution was measured using the spectrofluorometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of Ag/CDs in aqueous solutions
ce:section.ce:para: Initially, UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra were recorded for the TC stock solution (Fig. S1 A) while the excitation wavelength was set at 350 nm. The fluorescence peak is observed at 540 nm. Fig. 2 A shows the UV absorption and fluorescence emission spectra of CDs and Ag/CDs aqueous solutions. The CDs peak in the range of 250–330 nm is due to π-π * transitions of CC bonds in aromatic compounds and n-π * transfers of CO bonds [36]. In absorption spectrum of Ag/CDs, a new peak appeared at 420 nm due to the presence of silver. In addition, the increase in the intensity of the corresponding absorption spectrum is probably due to the increase in LSPR.

Fig. 2 A shows the fluorescence spectra in which the emission intensity of Ag/CDs is higher than that for CDs. From the comparison of fluorescence emission of colloidal solutions of CDs and Ag/CDs under UV lamp, it can be concluded that the fluorescence of CDs enhances in the presence of silver (Fig. 2A, inset). The results arise from the effect of local field enhancement generated near metallic surface (LSPR). The strongly localized electrical field of Ag nanoparticles plays the role of an antenna where nearby CDs are exposed to greater light intensity.

To report whether the emission intensity of the synthesized Ag/CDs depends on the excitation properties, different excitation wavelengths were evaluated in the range of 350–450 nm. The fluorescence intensities decrease with increasing the excitation wavelength up to 450 nm (Fig. 2B) and corresponding peaks shift to shorter wavelengths. The shape of spectrum for the Ag/CDs is asymmetric which is probably due to the presence of multiple PL peaks and several electronic transmission paths in the Ag/CDs electronic structure.

The XRD pattern of Ag/CDs exhibits the characteristic peak at 2θ = 19.30° related to (100) plane of CDs as well as the peaks at 2θ = 38.28, 44. 33, 64.58, and 77.48°, which are in accordance with the (111), (200), (220) and (311) lattice planes of Ag crystallized in face-centered cubic network (Fig. 2C) [37]. The XRD pattern of Ag/CDs has good conformity with reported literature [38–40]. In the XRD, the characteristic peak of β-CD is not observed which is due to its noncrystalline structure. Therefore, it does not affect the crystalline peak of silver.

Fig. 2D shows the FT-IR spectra of Malva extract, β-CD, and the colloidal Ag/CD nanoparticles. Wide bands between 3388 and 3424 cm−1 is due to OH vibrational stretching modes in alcohols. The small peaks at 2920 and 2926 cm−1 are due to the tensile vibrations of CH bonds. The peaks at 2364 and 1637 cm−1 with tensile vibrations of CC and CO are probably related to carbonyl or carboxylic acid. The peaks at 980 to 1200 cm−1 are related to the tensile vibrations of OCO and CO in polysaccharides. These functional groups are suitable for improving the hydrophobicity and stability of Ag/CDs in an aqueous solution [41–43]. After the reduction of silver ions, the intensity of tensile band around 3387 cm−1 decreases in the Ag/CDs spectrum and the peak of 1644 cm−1 is also transferred to 1592 cm−1.

In the β-CD spectrum, its “fingerprint” section (980 ~ 1200 cm−1) is observed [44]. The tensile bands are also visible at 3387 and 1634 cm−1, respectively. Both bands shift to 3354 cm -1 and 1637 cm−1, respectively, in the Ag/CDs spectrum. This observation suggests that the connection between Ag/CDs synthesized by Malva extract and β-CD molecules is due to functional group of OH which has already been reported. In addition, due to the decrease in the intensity of bending peak of OH and CO at 3387 and 1644 cm−1, it can be concluded that surface hydroxyl groups contribute in reducing process of Ag+ to Ag0 species. The presence of functional groups is also suitable for improving the hydrophobicity and stability of Ag/CD in aqueous solution [45,46].

The morphology and size of the prepared Ag/CDs was characterized using FESEM and TEM. FESEM image of colloidal Ag/CDs solution deposited on glass shows the formation of nanoparticles with size in the range from 30 to 60 nm (Fig. 3 A). In addition, TEM image (Fig. 3B) shows that Ag/CDs are nearly spherical in shape with narrow size distribution of particles. Due to higher atomic weight of silver compared to carbon, it seems darker in TEM image. The size histogram of Ag/CD particles is also presented in Fig. S1 B.

The EDS analysis was used to investigate the elemental composition of Ag/CDs deposited on glass. According to Fig. 3C, the EDS confirms the presence of silver, carbon, and oxygen in Ag/CDs. In addition, weight percent of different elements are presented in Table S1. For more information, the mapping analysis was performed on the sample and results showed the homogeneous distribution of O, C and Ag on the sample (Fig. S2). In other hand, the origin of Si and O elements in spectrum is from the substrate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of conditions for Ag/CDs synthesis
ce:section.ce:para: The conditions for synthesis of Ag/CDs were studied using various parameters such as the effect of AgNO3, Malva Sylvestris extract and β-CD concentrations, pH, temperature, and reaction time.

Fig S3A shows the UV–Vis spectrum of the solution containing Ag/CDs prepared at different AgNO3 concentrations in the range of 4 to 6 mM. As the concentration increases to 5 mM, the absorption intensity enhances. According to the Fig S3 B, at concentrations greater than 5 mM, the color of the colloidal solution turns brown, then the nanoparticles aggregate in an hour (Fig. S3 B). Therefore, 5 mM of the AgNO3 solution was selected as the optimal concentration for the synthesis of Ag/CDs.

The effect of different extract concentrations was investigated through addition of AgNO3 (1 mL, 5 mM) to the solutions and recording the absorption spectra (Fig. S4). When the concentration of Malva extract was increased from 0.0006 to 0.003 g mL−1, it leads to an increase in the absorption intensity of the solution containing Ag/CDs and a change in the wavelength of LSPR peak from 400 to 440 nm. Therefore, it seems that higher concentration of extract increases the reduction rate of silver ions by hydroxyl groups in anthocyanin and also the carbonization process for the CD synthesis. However, with addition of the extract concentration from 0.003 to 0.006 g mL -1, small change was observed in the intensity of absorption spectrum. Therefore, the optimized concentration of extract was selected 0.003 g mL−1.

β –CD has the role of stabilizer and size controller at the synthesis of Ag/CDs [47]. For this purpose, the β -CD concentration was changed from 0.3 to 0.8 g L−1 during the synthesis process. Fig. S5 A shows that by changing the β-CD concentration from 0.3 to 0.5 g L−1, the absorption intensity increases and then, it drops. The decrease of absorption intensity in higher β-CD concentration may arise from the transfer of the extracted compounds from water to β-CD cavity. Therefore, 0.5 g L−1 of β –CD was selected as optimum concentration.

The pH of precursor solution is an important factor which affects the size and shape of the synthesized nanoparticles [48]. It was observed that at pH of less than 5, a small number of Ag/CDs were formed. It is probably due to the weakening of the interaction of OH functional groups at anthocyanins with silver ions. As the pH of the reaction medium was changed from 6 to 9, more nanoparticles appeared in the solution and the absorption intensities increased (Fig. S5 B). However, at pH
>
7, the agglomeration of nanoparticles was occurred in the solution due to the increase in their sizes within 15 min and therefore, pH
=
7 was selected for synthesis of colloidal nanoparticles.

The effect of reaction time on synthesis of Ag/CDs was investigated in three periods of 2, 2.5 and, 3 h (Fig. S6 A). From spectra, the duration of 2.5 h was selected as the optimal time, because the corresponding spectrum has lower peak width in the wavelength about 410 nm, which indicates the formation of nanoparticles with a smaller size distribution. Although increasing the synthesis time causes to completion of the process, the colloidal particles have tendency to coalesce in the prolonged synthesis.

In addition, the effect of reaction temperature was investigated at three values of 80, 100 and, 120 °C. Fig S6B shows that with increasing the temperature, the intensity of the Ag/CDs absorption spectrum increases which is due to further formation and growth of nanoparticles in solution. The reduction of the Ag+ ions by reducing agents creates colloidal Ag0 nanoparticles. Increasing the temperature to 120 °C causes the conversion of colloidal silver nanoparticles in the liquid phase to solid-state. Therefore, in order to prepare higher amount of colloidal nanoparticles and prevent their agglomeration, the synthesis was performed at 100 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability of the Ag/CDs in aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the stability of Ag/CD solution, the effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity was investigated. The pH of the solution was adjusted using Tris buffer in the range of 4–11. As shown in Fig. S7, the pH of Ag/CDs solution in the range of 6.0 to 10 has small impact on the intensity of fluorescence. For more information, the fluorescence intensity versus time was plotted for the solution containing 5% of Ag/CDs in pH = 9. It can be observed that the intensity is almost constant which indicates the significant stability of nanoparticles in solution (Fig. S8).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of Ag/CD volume ratio
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of Ag/CDs concentration on the fluorescence intensity of TC (100 ppm) in aqueous solution was investigated. Different volume ratios of Ag/CD (0, 10, 25, 40, 70 and 100%) were added to TC solutions and related fluorescent spectra were recorded (Fig. S9 A). The shape and location of fluorescence peaks are the characteristic of TC emission spectrum. At 540 nm, the fluorescence intensity of TC increases with addition of volume ratio of Ag/CDs up to 70%. The enhanced fluorescence of TC was owing to the LSPR effect of Ag nanoparticles. It can be observed that at 100% volume ratio of Ag/CDs, the intensity of TC fluorescent peak is decreased which is probably due to the molecular self-absorption [49]. Fig. S9 B shows the images of the change in fluorescent intensity of solutions containing different volume ratios of Ag/CDs and 100 ppm of TC under UV illumination (365 nm). Although the fluorescence intensity increases with Ag/CDs ratio, in lower concentration of TC and in the linear range, better performance was observed at 5%. For this aim, other investigation was performed at 5% volume ratio of Ag/CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of analytical conditions for the determination of TC
ce:section.ce:para: To identify the optimal conditions for TC determination, various factors that likely play more decisive role in the diagnosis of target and analysis were investigated. They include the effect of pH, temperature, and time.

In this regard, pH of TC (1.0 mL, 0.5 μM) in the presence of Ag/CDs (volume ratio of 5%) was adjusted using buffer solution at the range of 6 to 10 and related fluorescence spectrum was recorded. According to Fig S10A, the maximum response of TC was obtained at pH = 9, which is probably related to the functional groups on Ag/CDs surface and the electrostatic interaction among them with the structure of analyte. As seen in Fig S10B, the intensity of fluorescence emission increases for 30 min, but after that, no change in the intensity of emission occurs. Also, according to Fig S10C, with a change in temperature from 25 to 60 °C, a slight change in the intensity of emission was observed. Therefore, pH = 9, temperature of 50 °C and time of 30 min were chosen as the optimal factors in the experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism for fluorescence enhancement process
ce:section.ce:para: From the obtained results, it can be concluded that there is an interaction between Ag nanoparticles and CDs, which leads to increased fluorescence intensity. The attachment or trapping of fluorophores (CDs) by β-CD as a spacer and stabilizer at the surface of Ag nanoparticles causes CDs to be at a reasonable distance from Ag to strengthen the fluorescence. Three energy transfer mechanisms have been proposed for plasmonic amplification: photon excitation, FRET and, plasmon-induced resonance energy transfer (PIRET). In Fig. 2A, the Ag/CDs absorption spectrum shows some changes compared to that for the CDs. The Ag absorption band appears at about 420 nm and also the absorption band at about 300 nm increases. This enhanced effect is created by the CDs in contact with Ag. Excited LSPR electrons produced in plasmonic metal nanoparticles can be effectively transferred to semiconductor CDs.

Under visible light, LSPR electrons are produced and enriched on the surface of silver nanoparticles. Increasing the electron density at the excited energy level of Ag facilitates the easy transfer of LSPR electrons (hot electrons) from silver to the CD. The transition of electrons from excited state to ground state of CDs causes to light emission. In addition, the possibility of trapping light and the PIRET process associated with CDs leads to an increase in the production of more energetic electrons in the conduction band (CB), and potentially increases the kinetics of relaxation to the valence band (VB) and increases the propagation per unit time. In this case, the quantum performance of fluorescence increases [50].

On the other hand, the presence of Ag metal nanoparticles near fluorophore (TC) increases the florescence intensity which is due to plasmon coupling between the metal and fluorophore. Ag nanoparticles could effectively improve the optical properties of TC foluorophore through MEF phenomenon (Fig. S11). The presence of metal near fluorophore (TC) increases their excitability and emission and create an increased quantum yield and photostability at optimal distance of 5–90 nm [51]. If plasmon and fuorophore are at the right distance, there is a transfer of energy between them.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ag/CDs as a sensor for determination of TC
ce:section.ce:para: The results suggest that Ag/CDs can be used as a probe to detect TC. The absorption and emission spectra of Ag/CDs synthesized by Malva sylvestris were recorded in aqueous solutions with different concentrations of TC. In optimum situations, various concentrations of TC were added individually into the solutions of Ag/CDs and mixed for 30 min in round-bottom flask. As shown in Fig. 4 A, with increasing TC concentration in the solution, the peak height gradually increase and the displacement at the LSPR peak location was observed from 420 to 380 nm. In addition, according to Fig. 4B, there is a linear relationship between ΔA and TC concentration in the range of 0.025 to 2 ppm

The fluorescence spectra of Ag/CDs solution in the presence of different TC concentrations are shown in Fig. 4C which show the intensity increment with addition of analyte. There is a linear relationship between F/F0 and TC concentrations in the range of 0.025 to 3 ppm (y = 7.9484x + 2.0834, R2 = 0.9924). Fig. 4D also shows the calibration curve for TC solutions. The limit of detection (LOD) is 0.6 μg L−1 (1.2 nM), which is estimated based on 3Sb/b (Sb standard deviation and b slope of the calibration curve).

Table 1 also compares the obtained results with that reported in other works for TC determination which shows the good performance of the proposed sensor. Furthermore, the relative standard deviation (RSD) for five determinations of 1 ppm of TC solution in the presence of Ag/CDs (5%) was 1.1%.

To evaluate the selectivity of Ag/CDs for detection of TC, some cations and anions such as Fe2+, Cu2+, Ca+, K+, Na+, Cl-, Br- and NO3 – (15 μM), other antibiotics and drugs including doxycycline, ciprofloxacin, amoxicillin, lincomycin, dopamine, nortriptyline (5 μM) were examined under optimized situation. According to Fig. S12, TC antibiotic displays more selectivity to the fluorescent probe than other antibiotics or chemicals.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of TC in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the method for real sample analysis, retrieval tests were performed using Ag/CDs to determine TC in diluted human blood plasma. For this aim, specific concentration of the antibiotic was injected into three samples of plasma diluted with Tris buffer (pH = 9.0, 0.01 M) and then the recoveries of TC were measured. As can be seen in table 2 , the results show that the developed fluorometric method can be used to determine the TC in real samples with appropriate accuracy.

In this study, the green synthesis of Ag/CDs from Malva Sylvestris extract was performed using hydrothermal method with a quantum yield of 9.92%. The prepared Ag/CDs were used in fluorescence determination of TC in aqueous solutions and in human plasma samples. Based on the results, it was confirmed that the increased fluorescence in the presence of Ag/CDs is due to the LSPR effect of silver nanoparticles. Additionally, the benefits of MEF using the available silver nanoparticles are promising for many applications based on the enhanced fluorescence properties. Experimental results showed that Ag/CDs are able to successfully determine TC with LOD of 0.6 μg L−1 (1.2 nM). Therefore, this probe provides a quick and easy way to measure TC in real samples with good selectivity and sensitivity and may have many potential applications in measuring TC in water or biological samples.

Akram Moaddab: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Visualization, Validation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Shahram Ghasemi: Supervision, Validation, Conceptualization, Methodology, Visualization, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2021.106222.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
